---
title: "The impact of COVID-19 on globalization"
subtitle: "Peer-reviewed study (One Health, 2020): a multi-criteria country-vulnerability index for the early pandemic"
description: "A peer-reviewed One Health study measuring COVID-19's effect on global mobility, trade, and economies, and ranking country vulnerability with a TOPSIS multi-criteria index."
---

::: {.tags .project-tags}
[Global health]{.tag} [Infectious disease]{.tag} [Multi-criteria analysis]{.tag} [TOPSIS]{.tag} [Co-authored]{.tag}
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Peer-reviewed.** Shrestha N, …, **Wardrup R**, …, Haque U. *The impact of
COVID-19 on globalization.* *One Health* 11:100180 (2020).
[doi:10.1016/j.onehlt.2020.100180](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.onehlt.2020.100180).
Co-authored while I was in the Department of Biostatistics and Epidemiology at
the University of North Texas Health Science Center.
:::

## Problem

In early 2020, COVID-19 was moving through exactly the networks that define
globalization: air routes, shipping lanes, cruise itineraries, and integrated
supply chains. The same interconnection that drives economic growth was
accelerating the spread, and the containment response (travel bans, lockdowns,
event cancellations) was in turn hitting the global economy hard. The paper set
out to measure both directions of that relationship, and to ask which countries
were most vulnerable in the early pandemic.

## Approach

We assembled evidence across three dimensions and compared pre-pandemic
baselines with early-pandemic conditions:

- **Mobility.** Passenger and trade flow through major airlines, seaports, and
  cruise lines.
- **Economy.** Trade volumes, workforce and unemployment, stock values, and
  event cancellations.
- **Healthcare capacity.** Health-system preparedness and response across
  countries.

Then, rather than ranking countries by eye, we built a formal vulnerability
index.

## Methods

- **Data sources.** Airline and cruise annual reports (2018–2019), the World
  Shipping Council, WHO situation reports, the Johns Hopkins dashboard, the
  Global Health Security Index, World Bank open data, and Index Mundi.
- **Vulnerability index.** A country-level index built with **TOPSIS** (Technique
  for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution), a multi-criteria
  decision method that scores each country by how close it sits to the
  best- and worst-case profiles across several weighted criteria.
- **Criterion weights.** Derived from a generalized linear model, over criteria
  including population density, days since the first reported case, the Global
  Health Index, diabetic prevalence, active and total cases, and testing volume.

## Findings

- Steep contractions in mobility: global airline passengers roughly halved, and
  major seaports and cruise lines saw sharp reductions, with near-universal
  travel restrictions.
- Large economic and workforce effects, including U.S. unemployment rising to
  14.7% in April 2020 and multi-billion-dollar losses from event cancellations
  and supply-chain disruption.
- A country-vulnerability ranking that put Singapore, Hong Kong, Uruguay,
  Bahrain, Maldives, Malta, Nepal, India, China, and Bangladesh among the most
  vulnerable, giving policymakers a comparable basis for prioritizing control
  measures.

## My contribution

I was a co-author from the UNTHSC biostatistics and epidemiology group and
contributed to the quantitative analysis behind the vulnerability assessment.
